name: Asturian

ancestors.notes: Primary descent from Hispanic Vulgar Latin, NW Iberian branch. Asturleonese diverged from Galaico-Portuguese roughly 9th–10th c. CE.

Gallaecian Celtic substrate: the Astures and neighbouring tribes of the Cantabrian coast shared cultural/linguistic affinities with the Gallaecian Celts. Castro culture influence; possible contribution to F-initial retention and prosodic patterns.
Suebi (Germanic) superstrate: the Kingdom of the Suebi (411–585 CE) controlled Gallaecia and extended into the Asturian-Cantabrian zone. ~200 lexical items and place-names.
Visigothic superstrate: pan-Iberian Germanic overlay after 585 CE. Less intense in the far north; Asturias was never fully under Visigothic control.
Andalusi Arabic: minimal direct contact given the north's brief or absent Moorish occupation. Some lexical borrowings through the Reconquista and repopulation movements.
Mozarabic adstrate: Romance of the Christian communities under Moorish rule in central and southern Iberia. Indirect contact through repopulation of the meseta.

notes: Modern Standard Asturian per the ALLA *Normes Ortográfiques* (8th ed., 2021). Spoken by ~100,000 speakers in the Principality of Asturias (Spain). KEY FEATURES distinguishing Asturian from Castilian: (1) ⟨x⟩ = /ʃ/ (voiceless postalveolar), not /x/ — 'xema' [ʃema] vs. Spanish 'jema' [xema]. (2) ⟨ll⟩ = /ʎ/ preserved (no yeísmo in standard). (3) Leonese diphthongs: Latin Ĕ → /ie/, Latin Ŏ → /ue/. (4) Latin F- preserved: 'falar', 'filu'. (5) Betacism: ⟨b⟩ = ⟨v⟩ = /b/. (6) No vowel reduction: 5-vowel system, unstressed vowels full. (7) Apico-alveolar /s/ [s̺] like Castilian. (8) Interdental /θ/ for ⟨z⟩ and ⟨c⟩ before e/i. (9) ⟨ch⟩ = /tʃ/ affricate. The family 'Asturleonese' also includes Leonese (nearly extinct in León), Mirandese (Miranda do Douro, Portugal), Rionorese and Guadramilese (Trás-os-Montes, Portugal). Sources: ALLA (2021), Cano González (2009), Penny (2002).
